plant_types.name: Cherry Tomato
plant_types.description: Small, sweet, bite-sized tomatoes.
plant_types.planting_notes: Great for snacking, grows well in containers.

plant_types.name: Grape Tomato
plant_types.description: Oblong, firm, and sweet, stores well.
plant_types.planting_notes: Ideal for salads, resistant to cracking.

plant_types.name: Beefsteak Tomato
plant_types.description: Huge, meaty, juicy tomatoes.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs support, long growing season.

plant_types.name: Brandywine
plant_types.description: Large heirloom with rich, sweet flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Prone to disease, needs staking.

plant_types.name: Roma Tomato
plant_types.description: Egg-shaped paste tomato with low moisture.
plant_types.planting_notes: Excellent for sauces and canning.

plant_types.name: San Marzano
plant_types.description: Classic Italian paste tomato with deep flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Grows well in full sun, needs support.

plant_types.name: Green Zebra
plant_types.description: Green-striped heirloom with tangy flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Harvest when slightly soft.

plant_types.name: Yellow Pear
plant_types.description: Small, yellow pear-shaped fruit with mild taste.
plant_types.planting_notes: Attractive in salads, grows prolifically.

plant_types.name: Black Krim
plant_types.description: Dark reddish-purple heirloom with smoky flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Prefers heat, benefits from regular watering.

plant_types.name: Mortgage Lifter
plant_types.description: Large, sweet beefsteak heirloom variety.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs staking and spacing.

plant_types.name: Sungold
plant_types.description: Bright orange cherry tomato, extremely sweet.
plant_types.planting_notes: Indeterminate, harvest regularly.

plant_types.name: Early Girl
plant_types.description: Medium-sized red tomato with early harvest.
plant_types.planting_notes: Good for cooler climates and short seasons.

plant_types.name: Celebrity
plant_types.description: Disease-resistant hybrid with balanced flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Determinate, good for slicing.

plant_types.name: Nantes
plant_types.description: Cylindrical, sweet, and tender carrot with little core.
plant_types.planting_notes: Best in loose, well-drained soil. Thin to 2 inches apart.

plant_types.name: Danvers
plant_types.description: Broad-shouldered carrot that tolerates heavy soil.
plant_types.planting_notes: Good for clay soils. Direct sow in early spring.

plant_types.name: Imperator
plant_types.description: Long, slender carrot common in grocery stores.
plant_types.planting_notes: Requires deep, loose soil. 70-80 days to maturity.

plant_types.name: Fuji
plant_types.description: Crisp, sweet apple with a long shelf life.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs cross-pollinator. Full sun, well-drained soil.

plant_types.name: Gala
plant_types.description: Mild, sweet apple that ripens early.
plant_types.planting_notes: Early harvest variety. Plant with pollinizer.

plant_types.name: Granny Smith
plant_types.description: Tart green apple excellent for baking.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs long growing season, best in warmer zones.

plant_types.name: June-bearing
plant_types.description: Produces one large crop in early summer.
plant_types.planting_notes: Plant in rows. Remove runners first year for stronger plants.

plant_types.name: Everbearing
plant_types.description: Produces two to three crops throughout season.
plant_types.planting_notes: Remove first-year blossoms through June for better yield.

plant_types.name: Day-neutral
plant_types.description: Produces fruit throughout the season regardless of day length.
plant_types.planting_notes: Excellent for containers, consistent production.

plant_types.name: Sweet Basil
plant_types.description: Classic culinary basil with large, fragrant leaves.
plant_types.planting_notes: Start indoors 6 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after frost.

plant_types.name: Thai Basil
plant_types.description: Spicier basil with anise notes, used in Asian cuisine.
plant_types.planting_notes: More heat tolerant than sweet basil, full sun.

plant_types.name: Purple Basil
plant_types.description: Ornamental basil with deep purple leaves and mild flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Decorative and edible, pinch flowers for continued leaf production.

plant_types.name: Russet
plant_types.description: Large, starchy potato ideal for baking and frying.
plant_types.planting_notes: Plant seed potatoes 4 inches deep, 12 inches apart, hill soil as plants grow.

plant_types.name: Yukon Gold
plant_types.description: Yellow-fleshed, buttery flavor, all-purpose potato.
plant_types.planting_notes: Earlier maturing than Russet. Good for roasting and boiling.

plant_types.name: Red Pontiac
plant_types.description: Red-skinned, white-fleshed potato with waxy texture.
plant_types.planting_notes: Good for boiling and salads. Stores well in cool conditions.

plant_types.name: Sweet Corn
plant_types.description: High sugar content, eaten fresh off the cob.
plant_types.planting_notes: Plant in blocks not rows for wind pollination. Sow after last frost.

plant_types.name: Dent Corn
plant_types.description: Field corn with dented kernels when dry, used for flour.
plant_types.planting_notes: Grows taller than sweet corn, harvest when fully dry.

plant_types.name: Popcorn
plant_types.description: Small, hard-kerneled corn that pops when heated.
plant_types.planting_notes: Let fully dry on stalk before harvesting. Isolate from sweet corn.

plant_types.name: Slicing
plant_types.description: Long, smooth-skinned cucumber for fresh eating.
plant_types.planting_notes: Direct sow after frost. Trellis for better fruit shape and airflow.

plant_types.name: Pickling
plant_types.description: Short, blocky cucumber with thin skin, bred for pickling.
plant_types.planting_notes: Harvest small for best results, check plants daily.

plant_types.name: English
plant_types.description: Long, seedless, thin-skinned cucumber, often sold wrapped.
plant_types.planting_notes: Best grown in greenhouse or indoors, parthenocarpic variety.

plant_types.name: Romaine
plant_types.description: Upright, crisp lettuce with elongated heads.
plant_types.planting_notes: Tolerates more heat than other types. Harvest outer leaves first.

plant_types.name: Butterhead
plant_types.description: Soft, tender leaves with buttery texture and mild flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Bolt-resistant variety. Harvest whole head or individual leaves.

plant_types.name: Iceberg
plant_types.description: Dense, crunchy heads with mild flavor, good shelf life.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs cool temperatures to head properly. Start indoors in summer.

plant_types.name: Softneck
plant_types.description: Mild, long-storing garlic commonly found in stores.
plant_types.planting_notes: Plant cloves in fall 1-2 inches deep. Harvest when lower leaves brown.

plant_types.name: Hardneck
plant_types.description: Stronger flavor with visible scape, fewer cloves per bulb.
plant_types.planting_notes: Remove scapes in summer to direct energy to bulb.

plant_types.name: Elephant Garlic
plant_types.description: Very large, mild-flavored cloves, technically a leek relative.
plant_types.planting_notes: Plant 4 inches deep, space 12 inches apart. Milder than true garlic.

plant_types.name: Yellow Onion
plant_types.description: All-purpose onion with strong flavor that mellows when cooked.
plant_types.planting_notes: Start from sets or transplants. Long-day variety for northern zones.

plant_types.name: Red Onion
plant_types.description: Mild, sweet onion often eaten raw, colorful in salads.
plant_types.planting_notes: Best fresh, does not store as well as yellow. Good for grilling.

plant_types.name: White Onion
plant_types.description: Sharp, pungent onion common in Mexican cuisine.
plant_types.planting_notes: Similar care to yellow onion. Harvest when tops fall over.

plant_types.name: Bell Pepper
plant_types.description: Sweet, blocky pepper available in green, red, yellow, and orange.
plant_types.planting_notes: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost. Needs warm soil to thrive.

plant_types.name: Jalapeno
plant_types.description: Medium-heat pepper popular in salsas and Mexican cuisine.
plant_types.planting_notes: Harvest green or red. Hotter when allowed to ripen fully.

plant_types.name: Habanero
plant_types.description: Very hot pepper with fruity, citrus-like flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs long warm season. Start indoors 10-12 weeks early.

plant_types.name: Calabrese
plant_types.description: Classic green broccoli with large central head and side shoots.
plant_types.planting_notes: Cool-season crop. Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost.

plant_types.name: Sprouting Broccoli
plant_types.description: Produces many small florets over a long harvest period.
plant_types.planting_notes: Overwinters in mild climates. Harvest side shoots regularly.

plant_types.name: Romanesco
plant_types.description: Striking fractal-patterned lime-green heads with nutty flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs consistent cool temps. More challenging than standard broccoli.

plant_types.name: Green Cabbage
plant_types.description: Dense round heads with mild flavor, staple in many cuisines.
plant_types.planting_notes: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Space 18-24 inches apart.

plant_types.name: Red Cabbage
plant_types.scientific_name: Brassica oleracea var. capitata f. rubra
plant_types.description: Purple-red cabbage with slightly peppery flavor, rich in anthocyanins.
plant_types.planting_notes: Same care as green cabbage. Color deepens in acidic soil.

plant_types.name: Napa Cabbage
plant_types.description: Elongated Chinese cabbage with mild, sweet flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Best as fall crop. Bolts quickly in warm weather.

plant_types.name: Snowball
plant_types.description: Classic white cauliflower with tight, compact curds.
plant_types.planting_notes: Blanch heads by tying leaves over curd. Needs consistent moisture.

plant_types.name: Purple Cauliflower
plant_types.description: Vibrant purple curds that turn green when cooked, high in antioxidants.
plant_types.planting_notes: No blanching needed. Slightly more cold-tolerant than white varieties.

plant_types.name: Cheddar Cauliflower
plant_types.description: Orange-colored curds with mild flavor, high in beta-carotene.
plant_types.planting_notes: Same culture as white cauliflower. Color intensifies with cooking.

plant_types.name: Savoy Spinach
plant_types.description: Crinkly, dark green leaves with rich flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Direct sow in early spring or fall. Bolts in heat.

plant_types.name: Flat-leaf Spinach
plant_types.description: Smooth, spade-shaped leaves, easier to clean than savoy.
plant_types.planting_notes: Good for baby greens. Succession plant every 2-3 weeks.

plant_types.name: Semi-savoy Spinach
plant_types.description: Moderately crinkled leaves combining traits of savoy and flat-leaf.
plant_types.planting_notes: More bolt-resistant than savoy. Good disease resistance.

plant_types.name: Curly Kale
plant_types.description: Ruffled, dark green leaves with slightly bitter, peppery taste.
plant_types.planting_notes: Frost improves flavor. Harvest outer leaves first.

plant_types.name: Lacinato Kale
plant_types.description: Dark blue-green, bumpy leaves also known as Tuscan or dinosaur kale.
plant_types.planting_notes: Sweeter than curly kale. Excellent for soups and sauteing.

plant_types.name: Red Russian Kale
plant_types.description: Flat, fringed leaves with purple veins and sweet flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Very cold hardy. Tender enough for raw salads.

plant_types.name: Black Beauty
plant_types.description: Dark green, classic zucchini with prolific production.
plant_types.planting_notes: Direct sow after frost. Harvest at 6-8 inches for best flavor.

plant_types.name: Golden Zucchini
plant_types.description: Bright yellow zucchini with mild, slightly sweet flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Same care as green varieties. Easy to spot among foliage.

plant_types.name: Costata Romanesco
plant_types.description: Italian heirloom with prominent ribs and nutty flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Best harvested small. Outstanding flavor when sliced and grilled.

plant_types.name: Black Beauty Eggplant
plant_types.description: Classic large, dark purple globe eggplant.
plant_types.planting_notes: Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost. Needs warm soil above 70F.

plant_types.name: Japanese Eggplant
plant_types.description: Long, slender, dark purple eggplant with tender skin.
plant_types.planting_notes: Thinner skin means less bitterness. Harvest frequently for continued production.

plant_types.name: Rosa Bianca
plant_types.description: Italian heirloom with lavender and white skin, creamy flesh.
plant_types.planting_notes: Mild, non-bitter. Needs long warm season for best results.

plant_types.name: Detroit Dark Red
plant_types.description: Classic round, deep red beet with sweet flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Direct sow in spring or fall. Thin to 3-4 inches apart.

plant_types.name: Golden Beet
plant_types.description: Yellow-orange beet that does not bleed color, milder than red.
plant_types.planting_notes: Same culture as red beets. Does not stain like red varieties.

plant_types.name: Chioggia
plant_types.description: Italian heirloom with red and white striped interior.
plant_types.planting_notes: Best eaten young for pronounced stripes. Sweet and mild.

plant_types.name: Cherry Belle
plant_types.description: Classic round, red radish with crisp white flesh.
plant_types.planting_notes: Ready in 22 days. Direct sow every 2 weeks for continuous harvest.

plant_types.name: French Breakfast
plant_types.description: Elongated red radish with white tip and mild flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Quick growing, best in cool weather. Goes hot in heat.

plant_types.name: Daikon
plant_types.description: Large white Asian radish used in stir-fries and pickling.
plant_types.planting_notes: Best as fall crop. Needs deep, loose soil for long roots.

plant_types.name: Sugar Snap Pea
plant_types.description: Sweet, edible-podded pea with thick, crunchy walls.
plant_types.planting_notes: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil is workable. Provide trellis.

plant_types.name: Snow Pea
plant_types.description: Flat, edible-podded pea used in stir-fries.
plant_types.planting_notes: Cool-season crop. Sow 4-6 weeks before last frost.

plant_types.name: Garden Pea
plant_types.description: Traditional shelling pea with sweet, round seeds inside tough pod.
plant_types.planting_notes: Shell before eating. Provide support for vines.

plant_types.name: Bush Bean
plant_types.description: Compact green bean that does not require support.
plant_types.planting_notes: Direct sow after last frost. Harvest every 2-3 days for continued production.

plant_types.name: Pole Bean
plant_types.description: Climbing green bean that produces over a long season.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs trellis or poles. Higher yield than bush beans over the season.

plant_types.name: Lima Bean
plant_types.description: Large, flat, buttery bean also known as butter bean.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs warm soil above 65F. Long growing season required.

plant_types.name: Jersey Giant
plant_types.description: All-male hybrid producing thick, tender spears.
plant_types.planting_notes: Plant crowns 6-8 inches deep. Do not harvest first 2 years.

plant_types.name: Purple Passion
plant_types.description: Sweet, tender purple spears that turn green when cooked.
plant_types.planting_notes: Sweeter than green varieties. Same long-term care requirements.

plant_types.name: Pascal Celery
plant_types.description: Classic green celery with thick, crunchy stalks.
plant_types.planting_notes: Start indoors 10-12 weeks early. Needs consistent moisture and rich soil.

plant_types.name: Golden Self-Blanching
plant_types.description: Yellow-green stalks that self-blanch, milder flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Easier than green celery. Less bitter without manual blanching.

plant_types.name: Long Island Improved
plant_types.description: Compact stalks covered in tight, round sprouts.
plant_types.planting_notes: Start indoors in spring for fall harvest. Frost improves sweetness.

plant_types.name: Red Rubine
plant_types.description: Purple-red Brussels sprouts with nutty flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Same culture as green varieties. Color holds when lightly cooked.

plant_types.name: Purple Top White Globe
plant_types.description: Classic turnip with purple crown and white base, mild when young.
plant_types.planting_notes: Direct sow spring or fall. Harvest at 2-3 inches for mildest flavor.

plant_types.name: Tokyo Cross
plant_types.description: Small, white, fast-maturing turnip with sweet flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Ready in 35 days. Excellent for succession planting.

plant_types.name: Beauregard
plant_types.description: Orange-fleshed sweet potato, most popular variety in the US.
plant_types.planting_notes: Plant slips after soil reaches 65F. Needs 90-120 days of warm weather.

plant_types.name: Japanese Sweet Potato
plant_types.description: Purple skin with white, dry, sweet flesh popular in Asian cuisine.
plant_types.planting_notes: Same culture as orange varieties. Slightly longer growing season.

plant_types.name: Green Globe
plant_types.description: Classic large artichoke with meaty, tender heart.
plant_types.planting_notes: Perennial in zones 7-11. Start indoors in colder zones for annual harvest.

plant_types.name: Imperial Star
plant_types.description: Bred specifically for annual production in cold climates.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs vernalization. Start indoors 8-10 weeks before transplanting.

plant_types.name: Heritage Raspberry
plant_types.description: Everbearing red raspberry producing fruit in summer and fall.
plant_types.planting_notes: Plant bare root in spring. Produces on both first and second year canes.

plant_types.name: Autumn Bliss
plant_types.description: Primocane-fruiting red raspberry with large, flavorful berries.
plant_types.planting_notes: Produces on first-year canes. Can mow canes to ground in winter.

plant_types.name: Black Raspberry
plant_types.description: Dark purple-black berries with intense, complex flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Tip-roots to spread. Train on trellis, prune in late winter.

plant_types.name: Concord
plant_types.description: Classic American grape for juice, jelly, and eating fresh.
plant_types.planting_notes: Cold hardy to Zone 4. Needs strong trellis. Prune heavily in late winter.

plant_types.name: Thompson Seedless
plant_types.description: Green, seedless table grape also used for raisins.
plant_types.planting_notes: Best in hot, dry climates zones 7-9. Needs full sun and good airflow.

plant_types.name: Muscadine
plant_types.description: Thick-skinned Southern grape with musky, sweet flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Best in zones 7-9. Needs separate male and female vines for most varieties.

plant_types.name: Crimson Sweet
plant_types.description: Classic oblong watermelon with dark and light green stripes.
plant_types.planting_notes: Direct sow after frost in warm soil. Needs 80-90 days and lots of space.

plant_types.name: Sugar Baby
plant_types.description: Small, round icebox watermelon perfect for small gardens.
plant_types.planting_notes: Compact vines, matures in 75 days. Good for northern gardens.

plant_types.name: Charleston Gray
plant_types.description: Large, oblong watermelon with gray-green rind and red flesh.
plant_types.planting_notes: Disease resistant. Needs long, hot growing season.

plant_types.name: Hales Best
plant_types.description: Classic muskmelon with netted rind and sweet orange flesh.
plant_types.planting_notes: Direct sow after frost. Ripe when stem slips easily from fruit.

plant_types.name: Ambrosia
plant_types.description: Exceptionally sweet melon with thick, juicy, salmon-colored flesh.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs warm soil above 65F. Excellent disease resistance.

plant_types.name: Elberta
plant_types.description: Large, yellow freestone peach with classic flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Self-fertile. Needs 800 chill hours. Prune annually for best production.

plant_types.name: Redhaven
plant_types.description: Medium freestone peach, firm and excellent for canning.
plant_types.planting_notes: Cold hardy, good for Zone 5-8. Thin fruit for larger peaches.

plant_types.name: Belle of Georgia
plant_types.description: White-fleshed freestone peach with sweet, delicate flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Good disease resistance. Needs 850 chill hours.

plant_types.name: Bing Cherry
plant_types.description: Large, dark red sweet cherry, the most popular variety.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs cross-pollinator. Full sun, well-drained soil. Zone 5-8.

plant_types.name: Montmorency
plant_types.description: Classic tart/sour cherry for pies, preserves, and juice.
plant_types.planting_notes: Self-fertile. Cold hardy to Zone 4. Easier than sweet cherries.

plant_types.name: Rainier
plant_types.description: Yellow-red sweet cherry with exceptionally sweet, delicate flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs pollinator. Susceptible to rain cracking. Zones 5-8.

plant_types.name: Bartlett
plant_types.description: Classic yellow pear with sweet, aromatic flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs cross-pollinator. Pick before fully ripe, ripen off tree.

plant_types.name: Anjou
plant_types.description: Green pear with firm, juicy flesh and mild flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Long storage life. Needs cold period after harvest to ripen properly.

plant_types.name: Asian Pear
plant_types.description: Round, crisp pear with apple-like texture, eaten fresh.
plant_types.planting_notes: Ripens on tree unlike European pears. Thin fruit for larger size.

plant_types.name: Brown Turkey
plant_types.description: Hardy fig with brownish-purple skin and sweet, pink flesh.
plant_types.planting_notes: Cold hardy to Zone 7. Can grow in containers in colder zones.

plant_types.name: Black Mission
plant_types.description: Dark purple-black fig with rich, sweet flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Best in zones 8-10. Produces two crops per year in warm climates.

plant_types.name: Celeste
plant_types.description: Small, sweet fig with violet skin, also called Sugar Fig.
plant_types.planting_notes: Most cold-hardy fig variety. Closed eye resists insects and spoilage.

plant_types.name: Tuscan Blue
plant_types.description: Upright rosemary with intense flavor and blue flowers.
plant_types.planting_notes: Perennial in zones 8-10. Grow in containers in colder zones.

plant_types.name: Prostrate Rosemary
plant_types.description: Trailing rosemary ideal for ground cover or hanging baskets.
plant_types.planting_notes: Zones 8-10. Excellent drainage required, drought tolerant once established.

plant_types.name: Arp Rosemary
plant_types.description: Most cold-hardy rosemary, surviving to Zone 6.
plant_types.planting_notes: Hardy to Zone 6 with protection. Lighter flavor than Tuscan Blue.

plant_types.name: Common Thyme
plant_types.description: Classic culinary thyme with strong, earthy flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Full sun, well-drained soil. Perennial in zones 5-9.

plant_types.name: Lemon Thyme
plant_types.description: Citrus-scented thyme excellent with fish and poultry.
plant_types.planting_notes: Same care as common thyme. Slightly less cold hardy.

plant_types.name: Creeping Thyme
plant_types.description: Low-growing thyme used as ground cover between stepping stones.
plant_types.planting_notes: Tolerates light foot traffic. Excellent pollinator plant.

plant_types.name: Bouquet Dill
plant_types.description: Compact dill with large seed heads, good for pickling.
plant_types.planting_notes: Direct sow after frost. Self-seeds readily. Attracts beneficial insects.

plant_types.name: Fernleaf Dill
plant_types.description: Dwarf dill ideal for containers, slow to bolt.
plant_types.planting_notes: 18 inches tall. Great for windowsill herb gardens.

plant_types.name: Santo Cilantro
plant_types.description: Slow-bolting cilantro with large, flavorful leaves.
plant_types.planting_notes: Direct sow in cool weather. Succession plant every 3 weeks.

plant_types.name: Calypso Cilantro
plant_types.description: Very slow-bolting variety, longest leaf production of any cilantro.
plant_types.planting_notes: Best for warm-climate gardeners. Direct sow, do not transplant.

plant_types.name: Italian Flat-leaf Parsley
plant_types.description: Flat leaves with stronger flavor than curly, preferred for cooking.
plant_types.planting_notes: Biennial. Slow to germinate, soak seeds overnight. Start indoors or direct sow.

plant_types.name: Curly Parsley
plant_types.description: Tightly curled leaves, milder flavor, often used as garnish.
plant_types.planting_notes: Same culture as flat-leaf. More cold tolerant.

plant_types.name: Common Sage
plant_types.description: Classic gray-green culinary sage with strong, savory flavor.
plant_types.planting_notes: Perennial in zones 5-8. Well-drained soil essential. Woody after 3-4 years.

plant_types.name: Purple Sage
plant_types.description: Ornamental sage with purple-tinged leaves and same culinary use.
plant_types.planting_notes: Slightly less hardy than green sage. Beautiful in herb gardens.

plant_types.name: Common Chive
plant_types.description: Mild onion-flavored herb with hollow, grass-like leaves.
plant_types.planting_notes: Perennial in zones 3-9. Divide clumps every 3 years.

plant_types.name: Garlic Chive
plant_types.description: Flat-leaved chive with mild garlic flavor and white flowers.
plant_types.planting_notes: Perennial. Remove seed heads to prevent aggressive self-seeding.

plant_types.name: English Lavender
plant_types.description: Classic fragrant lavender with purple flower spikes.
plant_types.planting_notes: Full sun, excellent drainage. Hardy to Zone 5. Prune after flowering.

plant_types.name: French Lavender
plant_types.description: Serrated leaves with fragrant flower heads topped by bracts.
plant_types.planting_notes: Zones 8-11. Blooms nearly year-round in mild climates.

plant_types.name: Grosso Lavender
plant_types.description: Lavandin hybrid prized for essential oil production and strong scent.
plant_types.planting_notes: Zones 5-9. Larger than English lavender. Excellent for dried arrangements.

plant_types.name: Hard Red Winter Wheat
plant_types.description: High-protein wheat used for bread flour, planted in fall.
plant_types.planting_notes: Sow in fall for late spring harvest. Needs vernalization period.

plant_types.name: Hard Red Spring Wheat
plant_types.description: Spring-planted bread wheat with high protein content.
plant_types.planting_notes: Sow as early as soil can be worked. 90-120 days to harvest.

plant_types.name: Peppermint
plant_types.description: Strong, cooling mint used in teas and desserts.
plant_types.planting_notes: Very aggressive spreader. Grow in containers to control.

plant_types.name: Spearmint
plant_types.description: Milder, sweeter mint used in cooking and drinks.
plant_types.planting_notes: Spreads vigorously by runners. Partial shade is fine.

plant_types.name: Mammoth Sunflower
plant_types.description: Giant sunflower reaching 10-12 feet with large seed heads.
plant_types.planting_notes: Direct sow after frost. Stake in windy areas. Full sun.

plant_types.name: Dwarf Sunflower
plant_types.description: Compact 2-3 foot sunflower with fluffy double blooms.
plant_types.planting_notes: Great for containers and borders. Direct sow after frost.

plant_types.name: Jack O'Lantern
plant_types.description: Classic carving pumpkin with smooth, orange skin.
plant_types.planting_notes: Direct sow after frost. Needs 100+ days and lots of space.

plant_types.name: Sugar Pie Pumpkin
plant_types.description: Small, sweet pumpkin ideal for pies and baking.
plant_types.planting_notes: Better flavor than large pumpkins. 100 days to maturity.

plant_types.name: Highbush Blueberry
plant_types.description: Most common cultivated blueberry, 4-6 foot bushes.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs acidic soil pH 4.5-5.5. Plant two varieties for cross-pollination.

plant_types.name: Rabbiteye Blueberry
plant_types.description: Southern blueberry tolerating heat and drought better than highbush.
plant_types.planting_notes: Best for zones 7-9. Needs cross-pollination. Less chill hours needed.

plant_types.name: Triple Crown
plant_types.description: Thornless, semi-erect blackberry with large, sweet berries.
plant_types.planting_notes: Thornless canes. Needs trellis support. Productive in zones 5-9.

plant_types.name: Navaho
plant_types.description: Thornless, upright blackberry with firm, sweet fruit.
plant_types.planting_notes: Self-supporting canes, no trellis needed. Zones 6-10.

plant_types.name: Greek Oregano
plant_types.description: Intensely flavored oregano, the true culinary variety.
plant_types.planting_notes: Full sun, lean soil. More flavorful when slightly stressed.

plant_types.name: Italian Oregano
plant_types.description: Milder hybrid of oregano and marjoram with sweet undertones.
plant_types.planting_notes: Less cold hardy than Greek. Good in containers.

plant_types.name: Virginia Peanut
plant_types.description: Large-kerneled peanut, the type commonly roasted and sold in shells.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs 130-150 warm days. Plant after soil reaches 65F.

plant_types.name: Spanish Peanut
plant_types.description: Small, round peanut with reddish skin, used for peanut butter.
plant_types.planting_notes: Shorter season than Virginia type, about 120 days. Compact plants.

plant_types.name: Nonpareil Almond
plant_types.description: Most popular almond variety with thin shell and sweet kernel.
plant_types.planting_notes: Needs cross-pollinator. Best in zones 7-9 with dry summers.

plant_types.name: All-In-One Almond
plant_types.description: Semi-dwarf, self-fertile almond good for home gardens.
plant_types.planting_notes: Self-fertile but produces more with cross-pollination. Zones 6-9.

plant_types.name: English Walnut
plant_types.description: Thin-shelled walnut with mild flavor, most commonly sold commercially.
plant_types.planting_notes: Large tree, needs space. Produces juglone that inhibits nearby plants.

plant_types.name: Black Walnut
plant_types.description: North American native with bold, distinctive flavor and very hard shell.
plant_types.planting_notes: Produces heavy juglone. Keep 50+ feet from sensitive plants.

planting_seasons.notes: Short season, start early indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost.
Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost date.
Transplant after last frost, typically mid-April.
Earlier start possible, frost risk low by March.
Very early season possible. Also try fall planting in August.
Start 6-8 weeks before last frost. Good paste tomato for Zone 6 summers.
Transplant after last frost. Determinate, no staking needed.
Can direct sow in March in Zone 8.
Successive plantings every 3 weeks for continuous harvest.
Spring and fall crops possible. Successive planting every 3 weeks.
Spring and fall crops. Excellent in Zone 7 cool springs.
Long growing window. Fall crop often best quality.
Tolerates heavier soil. Thin to 2 inches for best roots.
Can do spring and fall plantings in Zone 7.
Wait until soil is consistently warm, above 60F.
After all frost danger passes. Or start indoors 4-6 weeks early.
After all frost danger. Longer season allows multiple successions.
Very long basil season. Pinch flowers to extend harvest.
Excellent basil climate. Watch for bolting in peak summer heat.
Plant seed potatoes 4 inches deep after last frost. Hill soil as plants grow.
Can plant early March if soil is workable and not frozen.
Early start in Zone 7. Can do fall crop in August.
Earlier maturing than Russet. Good for spring planting.
Plant 2-4 weeks before last frost. Great early season potato.
Sow after last frost when soil reaches 60F. Plant in blocks for pollination.
April start if soil is warm. Succession plant every 2 weeks.
Good climate for corn. Plant in blocks minimum 4x4 for pollination.
Early season possible. Can also plant fall crop in August.
Direct sow after last frost. Trellis for best results.
Start after frost risk passes. Keep evenly watered to prevent bitter fruit.
Warm Zone 7 summers are excellent for cucumbers.
Early start possible. Second planting in July for fall harvest.
Start indoors 4-6 weeks before last frost. Tolerates light frost.
Spring and fall crops. Bolts in summer heat.
Early spring and fall. Avoid summer planting, bolt-resistant variety still needs cool temps.
Fall and winter crops also excellent in Zone 8.
Plant cloves 1-2 inches deep in fall for spring harvest.
Fall planting after first frost for best vernalization.
Plant in fall, harvest June-July. Long-storing variety.
Needs cold vernalization period. Remove scapes in summer.
Start indoors 10-12 weeks before last frost. Long-day variety.
Transplant 4-6 weeks before last frost. From sets is easiest.
Early start in Zone 7. Intermediate-day varieties may work better.
Plant bare root or crowns in spring. Remove runners first year.
Spring planting for first harvest following year.
Can also plant in fall in Zone 7 for spring crop.
Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost. Transplant after soil warms to 65F.
Start indoors early March. Transplant mid-May after frost danger passes.
Transplant after last frost in April. Warm soil is essential.
Long growing season allows early start. Can also direct sow in April.
Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost. Needs warm soil to transplant.
Transplant after frost. Harvest green or let ripen to red for more heat.
Good pepper climate. Consistent warmth produces best yields.
Very long season. Multiple harvests through fall.
Start very early, needs 100+ days. Transplant after soil is thoroughly warm.
Long warm season ideal for habaneros. Start indoors 10-12 weeks early.
Excellent habanero climate. Can produce well into November.
Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Cool-season crop.
Spring and fall crops. Transplant 2-3 weeks before last frost.
Early spring or late summer for fall harvest.
Best as winter crop in Zone 8. Heat causes bolting.
Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Transplant 2 weeks before last frost.
Spring crop transplanted early. Also good as fall crop started in July.
Early spring crop. Start fall crop in August.
Winter crop in Zone 8. Too hot for summer growing.
Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Demanding crop, needs consistent care.
Spring or fall crop. Fall is often more successful.
Early spring or fall. Blanch curds by tying leaves over head.
Winter crop. Needs cool, consistent temperatures for good curds.
Sow as soon as soil is workable. Frost tolerant. Bolts in heat.
Spring and fall crops. Succession sow every 2 weeks.
Early spring or fall planting. Summer is too hot.
Fall and winter crop in Zone 8. Excellent cool-season green.
Direct sow 4-6 weeks before last frost. Very cold hardy.
Spring and fall crops. Frost improves sweetness.
Nearly year-round in Zone 7. Plant fall crop in August.
Fall and winter crop. Too hot in summer.
Direct sow after all frost danger. Harvest at 6-8 inches.
After frost. Prolific producer, check daily in peak season.
Earlier start in Zone 7. Very productive through summer.
Long season. Second planting in July for fall harvest.
Start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost. Needs very warm soil.
Transplant after soil reaches 70F. Use black plastic mulch to warm soil.
Good eggplant climate. Transplant after last frost.
Long warm season ideal for eggplant. Multiple harvests.
Direct sow 2-3 weeks before last frost. Succession plant monthly.
Spring through summer sowings. Fall crop matures before hard frost.
Long growing window. Good spring and fall crop.
Nearly year-round in Zone 8. Beets prefer cooler weather.
Sow every 2 weeks for continuous harvest. Ready in 22 days.
Very fast crop. Succession plant every 10-14 days.
Nearly year-round crop. Goes hot and pithy in summer heat.
Fall through spring crop. Skip summer, too hot.
Sow as soon as soil is workable. Tolerates frost. Provide trellis.
Early spring crop. Sow 4-6 weeks before last frost.
Early start in Zone 7. Also plant fall crop in September.
Winter/spring crop. Too hot for summer peas.
Sow after last frost when soil reaches 60F. Succession plant every 2 weeks.
After frost. Quick crop, 50-60 days to harvest.
Long season allows multiple succession plantings.
Very long bean season. Skip hottest weeks in midsummer.
Plant crowns 6-8 inches deep. Do not harvest for first 2 years.
Plant crowns in early spring. Perennial, produces for 15-20 years.
Early spring planting. Harvest season starts earlier in Zone 7.
Very early start. May struggle in extreme summer heat.
Start indoors 10-12 weeks before last frost. Needs consistent moisture.
Long growing season crop. Transplant after frost, keep well watered.
Start early indoors. Celery needs 120+ days of cool weather.
Fall crop in Zone 8. Summer is too hot for celery.
Start indoors for fall harvest. Needs 90-100 days. Frost improves flavor.
Start indoors in spring, transplant for fall harvest.
Direct sow or transplant for fall/winter harvest.
Late summer start for winter harvest.
Spring or late summer sowing. Fast crop, 45-65 days.
Spring and fall crops. Harvest young for mildest flavor.
Long growing window. Best in cool weather.
Fall through spring crop. Skip summer heat.
Plant slips after soil reaches 65F. Marginal zone, use black plastic mulch.
Plant slips after last frost. Needs 90-120 warm days.
Good sweet potato climate. Plant slips when soil is warm.
Excellent sweet potato zone. Long warm season.
Perennial in Zone 7. Plant divisions or transplants in spring.
Perennial. Produces for 4-5 years from one planting.
Annual production. Start indoors, needs vernalization period.
Bred for annual harvest in cold climates. Start early indoors.
Plant bare root in early spring. Summer and fall crops.
Spring planting. Everbearing, produces July and September.
Early spring planting. Good production in Zone 7.
Plant early. May struggle in extreme summer heat.
Plant bare root vines in spring. Needs strong trellis.
Spring planting. Prune heavily in late winter for best production.
Good grape climate. Harvest late summer to early fall.
Start indoors 3-4 weeks early. Transplant after soil is warm.
Direct sow after frost in warm soil. Needs 80-90 days.
Good watermelon climate. Needs full sun and space.
Excellent watermelon zone. Long, hot summers ideal.
Start indoors 3-4 weeks early. Short season, may need plastic mulch.
Direct sow after frost. Ripe when stem slips from fruit.
Good melon climate. Consistent water until fruit sets.
Excellent cantaloupe zone. Reduce water as fruit ripens.
Plant bare root in early spring while dormant. Needs 800 chill hours.
Spring planting of bare root trees. Prune annually.
Plant dormant trees in late winter. Fruit in mid-summer.
May not get enough chill hours in warm Zone 8 areas.
Plant bare root in spring. Needs cross-pollinator variety nearby.
Spring planting. Full sun, well-drained soil essential.
Late winter planting. Protect blooms from late frost.
Marginal zone for sweet cherries. May lack sufficient chill hours.
Plant bare root in spring. Needs cross-pollinator.
Spring planting. Pick before fully ripe, ripen indoors.
Late winter planting. Good pear climate.
Early planting. May need low-chill variety in warmest areas.
Plant in spring after frost. Can be grown against south-facing wall.
Excellent fig climate. Two crops per year possible.
Perennial in Zone 8. Plant in spring after frost risk passes.
Most cold-hardy rosemary. Mulch heavily in winter.
Perennial with winter protection. Well-drained soil essential.
Evergreen perennial. Drought tolerant once established.
Plant after frost. Perennial, returns each spring.
Spring planting. Well-drained soil in full sun.
Nearly evergreen in Zone 7. Drought tolerant once established.
Evergreen perennial. Can become woody, replace every 3-4 years.
Direct sow after frost. Self-seeds readily for next year.
Sow succession every 3 weeks. Bolts in heat.
Early spring sowing. Also good for fall planting.
Early sowing. Bolts quickly in warm weather.
Direct sow after frost. Succession plant every 3 weeks.
Cool-season herb. Bolts in summer heat, replant in fall.
Spring and fall crops. Slow-bolting variety helps extend harvest.
Fall through spring crop. Summer is too hot.
Slow to germinate, soak seeds overnight. Biennial herb.
Direct sow or start indoors. Hardy biennial.
Early start. Overwinters in Zone 7.
Fall planting for winter harvest. Grows well in cool weather.
Plant after frost. Perennial, well-drained soil essential.
Spring planting. Becomes woody after 3-4 years, replace.
Nearly evergreen in Zone 7. Excellent drainage required.
Evergreen perennial. May struggle in humid heat.
Sow as soon as soil is workable. Perennial, returns each year.
Early spring sowing or divide existing clumps.
Very easy to grow. Divide clumps every 3 years.
Nearly evergreen in Zone 8. Attractive purple flowers.
Plant after frost. Excellent drainage critical. Mulch in winter.
Spring planting. Avoid wet feet, amend clay soil with gravel.
Excellent lavender climate. Prune after flowering.
Thrives in Zone 8. Full sun, lean soil preferred.
Fall planting. Overwinters and harvests in late June.
Fall sowing. Vernalization occurs over winter.
Later fall sowing in warmer zones. Harvest late May to June.
Fall through early winter sowing. May need supplemental chill.
Sow as early as soil can be worked. 90-120 days to harvest.
Early spring sowing. Harvest in mid to late summer.
Plant divisions or transplants. GROW IN CONTAINERS to prevent spreading.
Very aggressive. Use buried containers or raised beds with barriers.
Nearly evergreen in Zone 7. Contain aggressively.
Year-round in Zone 8. Partial shade preferred in hot climates.
Direct sow after last frost. Full sun, well-drained soil.
After frost. Stake in windy areas. 70-80 days to bloom.
Good sunflower climate. Seeds mature in fall.
Early start possible. Full sun essential.
Sow after frost. Needs 100+ days and lots of space.
Late May sowing for October harvest.
Good pumpkin climate. Give plenty of space.
Early sowing. Powdery mildew common in humid areas.
Plant bare root in spring. Acidic soil pH 4.5-5.5 essential.
Spring planting. Amend soil with sulfur if pH is too high.
Good blueberry climate. Mulch with pine needles for acidity.
Choose low-chill varieties for Zone 8.
Plant bare root in spring. Thornless, needs trellis support.
Spring planting. Produces on second-year canes.
Good blackberry climate. Harvest mid to late summer.
Early planting. May need afternoon shade in hottest areas.
Plant after frost. Perennial, more flavorful in lean soil.
Spring planting. Full sun, well-drained soil.
Nearly evergreen in Zone 7. Harvest before flowering for best flavor.
Evergreen perennial. Drought tolerant once established.
Plant after soil reaches 65F. Needs 130-150 warm days.
Good peanut climate. Long warm season supports full maturity.
Plant bare root while dormant. Late frost can damage blooms.
Best almond climate. Needs cross-pollinator.
Plant bare root in spring. Large tree, plan for space and juglone zone.
Spring planting. Keep 50+ feet from sensitive plants.
Good walnut climate. Produces after 4-5 years.
Early planting. Some varieties need more chill hours.
